Tafseer:
The eloquence of the interrogative style:
Commentators have generally taken the word 'hal' (has there/is there) in the meaning of 'qad' (indeed) instead of its interrogative sense. However, I have found no precedent for this meaning in classical Arabic. I have considered some examples presented as evidence of this meaning, and in my view, 'hal' is used in the interrogative sense in those cases as well. However, just as the interrogative style is used for various meanings in our language, it carries various implications in Arabic as well. There is no need or room here to explain all of them. Some aspects have been discussed in the previous Surahs, and suitable occasions will arise in the subsequent Surahs. Here, remember that a highly eloquent use of the interrogative style is when one wants to make the listener acknowledge a truth that is obvious, yet they are practically ignoring it. For example, a mother says to her unruly son: 'Do you not remember when you were placed in my lap as a lump of flesh, and I turned my blood into milk to feed you and raised you to be a man!'
Reflecting on this, you will see that this is not a simple declarative sentence, but contains various implied meanings. For example:
o It is a reminder to the son of a great right that lies on him and which he cannot deny, yet his behavior is contrary to it.
o It contains various aspects of rebuke, anger, grief, and regret.
o It is a highly truthful complaint and a highly effective appeal.
All these meanings are generated by the interrogative style of this sentence. If you replace the interrogative style with a simple declarative style, all these meanings will vanish. The exact same is the case with the verse under discussion. The word 'hal' in it contains various meanings that will unfold with the progressive development of the subject. If you replace it with 'qad', this verse will become completely unsuitable as an introduction to the subsequent subject.
The opening verse of a classical poem states:
'Did the poets leave any place un-mended, or did you recognize the beloved's home after some تجسس (search)!'
This is an excellent opening, and its entire beauty lies in its specific interrogative style. If you replace 'hal' with 'qad', this beauty will completely vanish. The poet asks himself in eagerness: why did the desire to write this poem arise in my heart today? Did the previous poets leave any gap in poetry that I want to fill today, or have the ruins of the beloved's home rekindled the fire of love, whose right must be fulfilled! The meaning is: both things are true. There was a major gap in poetry that is to be filled by this poem, and the subject of describing the beloved's home remained incomplete despite all the efforts of past poets, and its right must be fulfilled today.
Our purpose here is not to explain the beauty of this opening, but to show that there is a vast difference between styles. From the perspective of meter, the verse could have been completed with the word 'qad', but from the perspective of meaning, its value would have been lost.
The addressees of this verse are those who denied the Resurrection and recompense. Addressing them, the Qur'an has placed this question before them: Is it not a fact that a period of time passed over man when he was not a thing mentioned, but was a insignificant creation crawling in water, clay, and dust? Yet, His power guided this insignificant creation through various stages, refining its capabilities until it became the most noble of all creation! This question is intended to stimulate man's power of thought so that he reflects: Why did His power exercise such care for him? Why did He bless him with these high capabilities? Was it merely so that he should eat, drink, and perish one day? Does he bear no responsibility regarding these capabilities? Does the One Who created him with such care have no right over him? These questions should arise in the mind of anyone who reflects on his own existence.
Man's own existence is closest to him, and everything in it invites him to reflect. The interrogative style of the verse intends to awaken this reflective thought: if God is hidden from his sight, his own existence is not; he can see the signs of His power, wisdom, justice, and mercy within himself. Similarly, if he reflects, it will become clear to him that although he has not seen the Resurrection yet, its testimonies and proofs within his own soul are so clear that he cannot deny them, provided he is not completely stubborn and deviated.

Inviting man to reflect further:
In the preceding verse, reference was made to man's dark past at the beginning of life. Now, reference is made to the various stages of his creation, every aspect of which is before him, pointing to the same reality to which the preceding verse pointed—namely, that man is created from a drop of semen, which His power guided through various stages until it became a human being possessing hearing, sight, and intellect; let man reflect whether the God Who displayed such wonders on a drop of semen will find it difficult to recreate him, and let him also reflect whether the All-Knowing and Wise God Who blessed an insignificant drop with the high capabilities of hearing and sight, enabling it to distinguish between good and bad, gratitude and ungratefulness, did so in vain, such that He will not bring a Day of questioning and recompense.
In 'from a sperm-drop mixture' (min-nutfatin amshaj), the word 'amshaj' is the plural of 'mashj' and 'masheej', which means a mixed and blended thing. Although 'amshaj' is plural, it belongs to the category of words that serve as attributes for singular nouns. The mixture of the sperm-drop can refer to its composition of various forces and elements, as well as the mixture of the male and female fluids. It should noted that where different elements and conflicting temperaments are mixed, maintaining such balance and harmony to produce a sound result is impossible unless carried out under the supervision of a Wise and All-Powerful Being. An accidental occurrence cannot produce such a wise result.
The meaning of 'ibtila'': Commentators have generally taken 'nabtaleehi' (We try him) in the meaning of expressing the cause—namely: We created man to test him. However, if it expressed the cause, it would have been preceded by the particle 'li' (for/to), whereas it is in the form of a circumstantial clause (hal). The meaning of a circumstantial clause is different from that of a cause. In our view, it is a circumstantial clause, meaning: We created man in this manner, guiding him step by step through various stages until He brought him to the status of a hearing and seeing creation.
'Ibtila'' literally means to try and test. When a person tests something, he turns it over and examines it from various aspects. From this arose the meaning of moving something from one state to another. Here, it is used in this sense. Some commentators have also preferred this meaning.
The various stages of man's creation: The various stages through which man's creation passed to reach completion are explained in the Qur'an. We present some examples:
(O people, if you should be in doubt about the Resurrection, then [consider] that We created you from dust, then from a sperm-drop, then from a clinging clot, and then from a lump of flesh, formed and unformed—that We may show you. And We settle in the wombs whom We will for a specified term, then We bring you out as a child, then [We develop you] that you may reach your [time of] maturity.) (Al-Hajj, 22:5).
These details appeared in Surah Al-Mu'minun as:
(And certainly We created man of an extract of clay. Then We made him a small life-germ in a firm resting-place. Then We made the life-germ a clinging clot, then We made the clot a lump of flesh, then We made the lump of flesh bones, then We clothed the bones with flesh, then We caused it to grow into another creation. So blessed be Allah, the best of creators.) (Al-Mu'minun, 23:12-14).
The stages detailed in these verses are pointed to briefly in the verse under discussion, and 'nabtaleehi' is used to express this gradual progress through these stages—showing that this drop had to cover many stages to become a gem, and at every stage, His power examined it to ensure the capability required for that stage had developed.
'And We made him hearing and seeing' (fa-ja'alnahu samee'an baseera) - This summarizes the entire care and planning: while initially man was completely insignificant in the form of water, clay, and dust, a stage came when Allah Almighty turned him into a creation possessing hearing and sight. In the aforementioned verse of Surah Al-Mu'minun, this is described by: (then We caused it to grow into another creation. So blessed be Allah, the best of creators).
'Samee'un baseer' is a comprehensive description of all the high qualities of man. Through these qualities, the capacity to distinguish between good and bad developed in him, making him worthy of being tested by Allah Almighty to see whether he chooses the path of goodness and becomes a grateful servant, or chooses the path of evil and becomes ungrateful. From this also emerges the inevitable consequence: those who appreciate their hearing and sight will be rewarded, and those who do not will be punished. If this were not to happen, what was the purpose of all the care His power spent on man's creation! (Footnote 1)
_____
Footnote 1: [This footnote likely refers to the commentary of related verses mentioned in the original text.]

The fruit of hearing and sight: This describes the fruit of making him hearing and seeing: We guided him to the path. 'Guiding him to the path' means showing him the path of goodness and evil, as stated elsewhere:
(And We showed him the two ways) (Al-Balad, 90:10),
and in Surah Ash-Shams:
(And inspired it [with discernment of] its wickedness and its righteousness) (Ash-Shams, 91:8).
By showing him both ways, man became a witness against himself regarding good and evil, leaving him with no excuse to choose the path of evil. This was described in the previous Surah as:
(Rather, man, against himself, will be a witness, even if he presents his excuses.) (Al-Qiyamah, 75:14-15).
The blessing of choice: 'whether he be grateful or ungrateful' (imma shakiran wa imma kafoora) - This describes his choice and will: Allah Almighty gave him the capacity to distinguish between good and evil and blessed him with choice, so that he may choose the path of goodness and become a grateful servant to receive His reward, or choose the path of evil and become ungrateful to face His punishment.

The inevitable consequence of distinguishing between good and evil:
This is the inevitable consequence of being blessed with the capacity to distinguish between good and evil: since Allah Almighty has blessed him with this capacity, He must inevitably reward those who choose gratitude and punish those who choose ungratefulness. If this were not to happen, this capacity would have been given in vain, whereas Allah Almighty is Wise, and His wisdom is pure from performing any vain work.
Punishment for the ungrateful: It is stated: since We blessed him with the capacity to distinguish between gratitude and ungratefulness, the grateful and the ungrateful will not be equal before Us, but We will deal with them differently. For the ungrateful, We have prepared chains, shackles, and a burning Fire. Their feet will be bound in chains, shackles will be placed on their necks, and they will be dragged and cast into Hell.

Reward for the grateful:
In contrast to the ungrateful, this describes the reward of the grateful servants, describing them as 'Abrar' (the righteous). The analysis of this word appeared in its relevant place. The core spirit of 'birr' (righteousness) is fulfilling covenants and responsibilities, and the core of 'shukr' (gratitude) is appreciating and fulfilling the right of blessings. Both share a clear common ground. The servants of Allah who appreciate His blessings and fulfill their rights are His loyal servants.
The analysis of the word 'ka's' (cup) appeared in its relevant place—showing that it refers to both the cup and the drink within it.
'Mizaj' means mixture. To add taste, fragrance, or balance to drinks, certain things are mixed with them. Arab poets mention such mixtures for wine. The mixture for the wine of the people of Paradise will be the water of the spring of Kafur.
'Kafur' here does not refer to the well-known camphor. The Qur'an itself clarifies that it is a spring of Paradise, on the banks of which the chosen servants of Allah will drink, doubling their joy through its mixture. As for the question of why it is named Kafur, while such questions do not arise regarding names, the mind inclines to the view that there must be some suitability between the name and the named. What is this suitability? This belongs to the allegorical verses, the actual reality of which will manifest on that Day to those fortunate servants who will be blessed with it.

Drinking on the banks of the spring:
In 'They drink of it' (yashrabu biha), the particle 'bi' is used in my view in the sense of 'in' [or 'at'], just like in 'they believe in the unseen' and 'who fear their Lord in the unseen'. The meaning is: this spring will be reserved specifically for the assembly of Allah's chosen servants. 'The servants of Allah' ('ibadullah) refers to the righteous (Abrar) mentioned above. They alone will be honored with having a spring reserved for their assembly. Note that drinking on the banks of a spring is highly pleasing.
'They cause it to gush forth abundantly' (yufajjeorunaha tafjeera) - 'Tafjeer' means making channels and streams of a spring to spread them. The meaning is: the people of Paradise will not need to travel to reach this spring; rather, they can make streams of it flow wherever they wish, enjoying its taste without any difficulty.

The deeds of the righteous for which they receive this honor: This describes the qualities and deeds for which they will be honored by their Lord.
Fulfilling vows (nadr) is highlighted among their qualities. Those who observe the vows they took upon themselves can be expected to observe the obligations their Lord placed on them to an even greater degree. Commentators have extended the meaning of 'nadr' to encompass all virtues, whether taken upon themselves or commanded by Allah, but this exceeds the literal meaning.
The importance of vows before Islam: Note that 'nadr' (vow) was of great importance in past religions, and the Arabs of Jahiliyyah also observed it with care. When they wanted to perform a virtue—whether Hajj, sacrifice, or spending—they would vow to do so and fulfill it. This was due to their unlettered state, which made them use vows to fill the gap of lacking explicit laws. After the Shariah was revealed, its scope became limited: polytheistic vows were prohibited, and those exceeding capacity were corrected. Since this Surah belongs to the period when the Shariah was not fully detailed, vows are mentioned with special care. Later, after the complete Shariah was revealed, its scope became limited.
Fear of the Hereafter: 'And they fear a Day whose evil will be widespread' (wa yakhafuna yawman kana sharruhu mustateera) - 'Mustateer' means widespread and universal. This describes their fear of the Hereafter—showing that they constantly feared a Day whose terror will affect everyone: great and small, rich and poor, ruler and subject, worshipper and worshipped. Only those whom Allah protects will be safe.

Helping the poor:
This describes their behavior toward His creation: they feed the poor, orphans, and captives despite their own need. 'It'am' (feeding) is not used in a narrow sense, but includes meeting other essential needs. The word is used in a wide sense in the Qur'an.
The reference of the pronoun in 'ala hubbihi' (despite love for it): Commentators have generally referred the pronoun to Allah, meaning: they feed the poor out of love for Allah. While grammatically correct, I prefer the view of those who refer the pronoun to 'food' (ta'am)—meaning: they feed the poor despite their own need and love for it.
There are several reasons to prefer this view:
First, this describes the character of the righteous (Abrar), and to attain 'birr' (righteousness), a person must spend what is beloved to him, whether because of its value or his own need for it, as stated:
(Never will you attain the good [reward] until you spend [in the way of Allah] from that which you love) (Al-Imran, 3:92),
and in Surah Al-Hashr:
(and give them preference over themselves, even though they are in privation) (Al-Hashr, 59:9).
Second, their reward is described further on as:
(And We rewarded them for what they patiently endured with a garden and silk [garments]) (verse 12).
Note that what demonstrates their 'patience' is that they fed the poor despite their own need. If 'ala hubbihi' is interpreted otherwise, there remains nothing to demonstrate their patience, whereas the discourse requires it. This establishes the reference of the pronoun.
Third, the spending of beloved wealth, prioritizing others over oneself, is what is truly for the sake of Allah. This automatically includes the meaning of love for God.
The mention of captives alongside the poor and orphans relates to the circumstances of the time of revelation, when prisoners had to ask for their needs. This has changed with modern prison systems, but helping prisoners and their families still falls under this command.

Spending solely for the sake of Allah: This describes the inner motive of their spending: they seek neither reward nor gratitude from those they help, but do so solely for the sake of Allah and out of fear of the Hereafter.

The Day of Recompense is described here as 'aboos' (harsh) and 'qamtareer' (distressful). 'Aboos' means harsh and dry, and 'qamtareer' is added to express intensity—showing that it will be such a harsh and distressful Day that no one will be able to help another. On that Day, everyone will face what they have done. God's mercy will turn only toward those who supported the poor and orphans, spending their wealth despite their own need.
This does not require them to say this verbally to those they help; rather, it describes the inner motive of their spending—showing that they do so solely for the sake of Allah, without any other motive.

This describes their reward: since they feared that harsh Day and spent their beloved wealth to protect themselves, Allah will protect them from its evil, and on that Day when faces are dark, their faces will be radiant and joyful.

The reward for patience:
And because they patiently endured, He rewarded them with a garden and silk. 'For what they patiently endured' (bima sabaru) refers to the patience demonstrated in: 'And they feed food despite love for it'. Only a person of patience can offer his food to another while experiencing hunger himself.
They will receive a 'garden' to enjoy its fruits and eternal ease, and 'silk' to wear. Thus, housing, food, and clothing are all included.

Not seeing the sun or freezing cold means they will be completely protected from the distress of heat and cold, sitting on thrones in Paradise. The sun will have light and life-giving power, but will lack heat and intensity. The climate will remain pleasant, moderate, and spring-like, without the blight of autumn or freezing winds.
That is, the shades of the gardens will cover them, and the clusters of fruits will hang low, within their reach. They will not need to exert any effort to obtain them.
That is, vessels of silver and cups of glass will be circulated around them, and this glass will be of silver, yet clear like glass.

The meaning of 'taqdeer':
'Qaddaruhaha taqdeera' - That is: these vessels and cups will be of different shapes, sizes, and measures, arranged by the servants with care and skill to suit every occasion, need, and drink. The word 'taqdeer' encompasses all these meanings.

'Zanjabeel' and 'Salsabeel':
Above, the spring of Kafur was mentioned; this mentions another spring: they will be given to drink a cup mixed with Ginger (zanjabeel). This is also a spring of Paradise, named 'Salsabeel'. Regarding names, we noted that they do not depend on literal meanings. 'Zanjabeel' literally means ginger, but names can be given based on some suitability. While we know the names of many things in Paradise and Hell, we cannot comprehend their actual reality; general belief is sufficient.
Its actual reality will be known on that Day. The other name of this spring is 'Salsabeel'. Al-Zajjaj interpreted it as flowing smoothly, which is a description of its flow.

The young servants and their qualities:
This describes the qualities of the young servants who will serve them. It is stated: they will remain of a youthful age forever. The analysis of 'mukhallad' appeared in its relevant place. This points to two matters: first, being young, they will be active and energetic; second, they will remain at this age forever, so their energy will remain constant, and they will be familiar with their masters' habits and taste. Note that good service depends on experience; an old servant has experience but lacks energy, while a young servant has energy but lacks experience. For the people of Paradise, Allah has arranged servants possessing every virtue eternally.
'When you see them, you would think them scattered pearls' (idha ra'aytahum hasibtahum lu'lu'am-manthoora) - This portrays their beauty, purity, and elegant appearance: when you see them, you would think they are scattered pearls.

That is, wherever you look, you will see a great blessing and a magnificent kingdom:
'The charm of the scene attracts the heart, saying: this is the place.'

The clothing of the people of Paradise:
In 'Upon them' (aliyahum), the word 'aliya' is in the position of a circumstantial clause (hal), referring to their outer garments—such as cloaks and robes.
Their outer garments will be of green silk (sundus) and heavy brocade (istabraq). Sundus and Istabraq were well-known Persian names for silk fabrics. Some have distinguished between fine and thick silk, but this is unnecessary. Here, it refers to the silk of Paradise, the actual reality of which is known only to Allah. Since the Arabs were familiar with the cultures of Persia and Egypt, the names of luxury items from those cultures were used metaphorically. The rulers of the time wore robes of sundus and istabraq. Obviously, if their outer robes are of such silk, their inner garments will be even finer. This portrays their outer garments, from which you can imagine the rest.
Respecting the taste of the people of Paradise: 'And they will be adorned with bracelets of silver' (wa hullooo asawira min fiddatin) - The rulers of the time wore bracelets of gold and silver. It is stated: they will be adorned with bracelets of silver. Here, silver bracelets are mentioned; in Surah Al-Kahf, gold bracelets are mentioned:
(They will be adorned therein with bracelets of gold and will wear green garments of fine silk) (31).
This same is stated in Surah Al-Hajj, verse 23, and Surah Fatir, verse 33. This represents diversity—showing that they will wear gold or silver according to their desire. Diversity of taste is a natural thing, and everyone's choice will be respected in Paradise: (They will have whatever they desire therein, and with Us is more) (Qaf, 50:35).
This is the common interpretation, but my mind also inclines to another view—namely, that the people of Paradise will have different ranks, such as 'the foremost' (sabiqun) and 'the people of the right hand' (as-hab-ul-yameen). The difference in their ranks and blessings is natural; hence, gold is mentioned in some places and silver in others.
A specific point: 'And their Lord will give them a pure drink' (wa saqahum rabbuhum sharabanto-tahoora) - Note the difference in the style of these three phrases in the Surah: 'They drink of a cup', 'They are given to drink a cup', and 'their Lord will give them'. Those with a taste for grammar can perceive this: it points to their rising through the ranks of proximity to Allah, until their Lord Himself gives them a pure drink! What is this pure drink? Its concept cannot be comprehended in this world; therefore, no metaphor is used for it. It is known only to He alone. It refers to the pure, sealed wine reserved for the chosen, described in Surah Al-Mutaffifeen as:
(They are given to drink of a pure wine, sealed. The seal of it is musk. And for this let those aspire who would aspire. And its mixture is of Tasneem, a spring from which those near to Allah drink.) (Al-Mutaffifeen, 83:25-28).

Praise from the Lord:
That is, receiving all of this, they will be praised by their Lord: 'This is the recompense for your deeds, and your effort has been appreciated!' For this, they do not need to be indebted to the intercession of anyone else. This contains a rebuke to those who relied on the intercession of their idols, who will not benefit them.

Exhorting the Prophet to patience and waiting:
Just as in Surah Al-Qiyamah, verse 16: (Move not your tongue with it to hasten [your recitation of] it) appeared, this verse appears here in the same context and for the same purpose. Having described the end of the deniers and the believers, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is exhorted to remain patient: continue your work, and wait for the decision of your Lord. What they are warned of will surely face them.
'Indeed, We have revealed to you the Qur'an progressively' (inna nahnu nazzalna 'alayka-l-qur'ana tanzila) - That is: you did not present this Qur'an on your own, nor did you request it, such that the responsibility to show them the punishment rests on you; rather, We are the One Who revealed it. Note the emphasis and majesty in 'Indeed, We' (Inna nahnu). The meaning is: since We revealed it, pay no attention to their opposition. Dealing with them is Our responsibility, and We are sufficient.
The word 'tanzil' (progressive revelation) points to the care exercised, showing that this Qur'an is not a human demand or a baseless statement that can be swept away by their opposition; rather, it is a Book revealed by Allah with care, the every word of which will be fulfilled in this world and the Hereafter.

'So be patient for the decision of your Lord and do not obey from among them a sinner or an ungrateful one' (fa-sbi-r li-hukmi rabbika wa la tuti' minhum athiman aw kafoora) - As noted, 'sabr' (patience) followed by the preposition 'li' implies waiting. The meaning is: since you did not request this Book, do not be anxious about their objections. Perform your duty of communication, and wait for the decision of your Lord. Pay no attention to those rebels who demand to see the punishment. 'Obey' (tuti') here means paying attention. This appeared in Surah Al-Alaq, verse 19: (No! Do not obey him. But prostrate and draw near).
'Athim' and 'Kafoor': Above in verse 3, the words 'grateful' (shakir) and 'ungrateful' (kafoor) appeared: (whether he be grateful or ungrateful). Here, in contrast to 'grateful', the word 'athim' (sinner) is used. This refers to the one who violates rights—both the rights of God and the rights of His creation. Violating the rights of His creation is described as 'athim', and violating the rights of God is 'kafoor'. Although both are linked—as one who violates the rights of creation cannot fulfill the rights of God—this tendency manifests differently in individuals. In some, greed and miserliness produce hard-heartedness, making them enemies of goodness (such as Abu Lahab); in others, arrogance and pride prevent them from accepting the truth (such as Abu Jahl). Keep both in view as you read: 'and do not obey from among them a sinner or an ungrateful one'. This points to the two categories of your opponents: those trapped in the love of the world, and those whose pride prevents them from accepting the truth; both are unworthy of attention, and their disease is incurable.

The remedy of patience:
This states the remedy for the patience commanded above: remember the name of your Lord morning and evening. Morning and evening can mean at all times, and 'remembrance' (dhikr) is general, encompassing both prayer and constant remembrance. 'And project of the night' points to Tahajjud, as detailed in the previous Surahs, particularly in Surah Al-Muzzammil.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25 and 26 was combined in the original text.]

The actual disease of the opponents:
To comfort the Prophet, this points to their actual disease: their objections regarding the warning are merely a fabrication to cover up the reality. Their actual disease is that they are worshippers of the immediate pleasures of this world. They lack the courage to sacrifice this cash for the promise of the Hereafter. Although that Day will be highly difficult, they raise baseless doubts regarding the Resurrection to cover up their worldliness, trying to convey that their refusal is based on some arguments, not on pride.

Threat to the opponents and answering their doubt:
This is a threat to the opponents, and answers their primary doubt regarding the Resurrection—namely: 'How is it possible to be resurrected after we have decayed and turned to dust?' It is stated: We created them and made their joints and muscles strong; so since We did this—which they cannot deny—We can easily restore their joints and muscles and resurrect them whenever We will. Since the first creation was not difficult for Us, why should the second be?
'Shadda asrahum' means joining the bones and muscles with strength. The similarity in 'We can substitute their likes' (baddalna amthalahum) refers to His power to recreate them in their actual form.

Warning and declaration of indifference:
This shows indifference to them: this warning is communicated solely for their own benefit. There is no benefit in it for Allah, nor any personal objective for the Messenger. Whoever wishes may accept it and choose the path to his Lord, and whoever rejects it should be prepared to face the consequence.

The divine law regarding the guidance to faith:
This points to the divine law regarding the guidance to faith—showing that Allah's every work is based on His wisdom and knowledge. He blesses with guidance only those who use their hearing and sight, and appreciate the capacity to distinguish between good and bad, as noted in verses 2-3. Those who ruin these capacities and become blind and deaf are not guided. For them, He has prepared Hell because they wronged themselves and made themselves deserving of it. Allah is ever Knowing and Wise; He does not wrong His servants.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 30 and 31 was combined in the original text.]
